content.full_text:
RAVI MEKTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
December 20,
1995
Mr.
Carl 0.
Waggoner
Law Offices of Waggoner
& Loeffler
318 McHenry Avenue
Modesto,
California
95354
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95-389
Dear Mr.
Waggoner:
You are the City Attorney for the City of Newman.
This is in
response to your request for advice regarding the conflict—of—
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
as
they pertain to City of Newman Mayor James Silveria and
Councilinember Robert Novoa.
1
QUESTIONS
1.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding the Police Activity League’s
(“PAL”)
proposed use of a
theater the city has recently acquired?
PAL,
which provides
activities for city and area youth, wishes to maintain a small
office in the theater building.
2.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding what type of programs the city will permit in the
building (i.e., movies, plays, musicals,
PAL events, craft fairs,
etc.)?
3.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding the interim use of the building (i.e., nonprofit groups,
social events)?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Cod,e of Regulations, Sections
18000—18995.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


-
Page
2
4.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding what improvements are to be made to the theater,
Such as
roof repair or a new air conditioner,
and is there a distinction
if the improvements are visible from the street?
5.
May either official participate in the decision regarding
payment of the existing trust deed or from where the payment
should come?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Neither official may participate in any decisions
regarding the theater if the decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on their economic interests.
From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there is
a substantial likelihood that permitting the PAL organization to
maintain a small office will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on either of the officials’ economic interests.
2 and 3.
Neither official may participate in decisions
regarding the type of programs the city will permit in the theater
if the programs will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on their economic interests.
We do not have
sufficient information about the uses being considered to give you
formal advice on this issue,
however,
we have provided guidance to
assist you in analyzing the issue.
2
The officials must carefully
analyze the impact of these decisions.
If the decisions will have
any financial effect on Mayor Silveria’s real property interest
within 300 feet of the theater,
he may not participate and if the
decisions will have a material financial effect on Mayor
Silveria’s tenant or business entity,
he may not participate.
Similarly, if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s tenant or
leasehold interest, then Councilmeniber Novoa may not participate
in the decisions.
4.
We do not have sufficient information about the
improvements being considered to give you formal advice as to
whether the officials may participate.
To the extent that
decisions pertaining to the theater are merely implementation
decisions which do not affect the existence of the project as a
whole and will not independently materially affect an economic
interest of the officials, the officials may participate.
5.
As noted above, the officials may not participate in
decisions regarding the theater if the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their economic
interests.
According to the facts you have provided, it appears
that the decision on the trust deed is an implementation decision
2
Please note,
informal assistance does not provide the reguestor
with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)


Page
3
which will not affect the existence of the project.
So long as
the decision will not independently materially affect an economic
interest of the officials, the officials may both participate.
PACTS
The City of Newman recently acquired the West Side Theater
through a Trustee’s Sale from a second deed of trust.
Mayor James
Silveria owns real property within 300 feet of the theater.
He
has a tenant located in his real property.
In addition, Mayor
Silveria maintains his construction business in the property.
City Councilmember Robert Novoa operates a jewelry store in
part of a building which he has leased.
The building is within
300 feet of the theater.
The theater will be used for certain city functions, although
the exact uses and the terms of such uses have yet to be
established.
An advisory committee was appointed to make
recommendations and report to the city council regarding the
theater’s use.
The report was accepted at the last city council
meeting, but Councilmember Novoa and Mayor Silveria abstained from
the decision.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or iore in value
provided to, received by or promied to the public


—--—-----
Page
4
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103.)
The mayor and councilmeinber have a variety of interests that
may be affected by decisions regarding the theater.
If any of the
decisions involving the theater reasonably and foreseeably will
have a material financial effect on the officials’
economic
interests, the officials must disqualify themselves from the
decisions.
In Part A of this letter, I will identify the economic
interests held by Mayor Silveria and Councilmember Novoa which may
be affected by decisions regarding the theater.
In Parts
B and C,
I will set forth the Commission’s regulations the officials will
need to apply to determine whether any decisions will foreseeably
and materially affect their financial interests.
In Part D,
I
will provide advice on how to apply the materiality regulations to
determine if the officials must disqualify themselves from any
decisions involving the theater.
A.
ECONOMIC
INTERESTS
1.
Mayor Silveria
Mayor Silveria has three potential interests in decisions
involving the theater.
(a)
Mayor Silveria has real property,
presumably worth more
than $1,000,
located within 300 feet of the theater.
(Section
87103(b).)
(b)
Mayor Silveria’s construction business is located in
his building near the theater.
(Section 87103(a).)
If the mayor
has an investment worth more than $1,000 in his construction
business,
he must analyze whether the decisions will reasonably
foreseeably and materially affect his business entity.
(c)
Mayor Silveria has a tenant in the building and that
tenant is a source of income to Mayor Silveria if the mayor has
received more than $250 from the tenant within the past 12 months.
(Section 87103 (c).)
2.
Councilniember Novoa
Councilmember Novoa has two interests which may be affected
by decisions regarding the theater.
(a)
Couricilmember Novoa has a real property interest in
the form of a leasehold,
assuming that his lease is more than a
month-to-month tenancy, in a building within 300 feet of the
theater.
(Section 82033; Regulation 1233.)


FIleNô. A9b-39
Page
5
(b)
Councilmember Novoa’s jewelry business is located in
the leased property near the theater.
If the councilmenther has an
investment worth more than $1,000 in his construction business,
he
must analyze whether the decisions will reasonably foreseeably and
materially affect his business entity.
(Section 18703(a).)
Thus,
each official is required to disqualify himself from
making or participating in any decisions which would have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on the official’s
economic interest.
B.
FORESEEABILITY
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there isa substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
C.
MATERIALITY
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which
provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial
effects of a decision are material.
These regulations apply
different standards depending on whether the decision will
directly or indirectly affect the official’s economic interest.
3
The effect of a decision on real property or a business
entity in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial
ownership interest,
is material if the official’s property is
directly involved in a decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3).)
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3)
provides a list of situations in
which an official’s real property is directly involved in a
decision.
Some examples include decisions to zone or rezone the
official’s property, decisions to annex .the property, decisions
concerning assessments on the property and decisions to include
the property in or exclude it from any city, county or other local
governmental decision.
Regulation 18703 provides an exception where the material
financial effect on the official’s interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.)
Since the official’s property is so close to
the theater, it does not appear that the test for the exception
will be met.
However,
we have enclosed the regulation for your
consideration.
If you do believe the 1
’public generally” exception
will apply or if you have any further questions, please feel free
to contact us at
(916)
322—5660.


•
File No
Page
6
The effect of a decision on a business entity is material i
the business entity initiates the proceeding, is a named party in
the proceeding or if the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of a permit,
license or contract.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
The officials will be disqualified if their property is
directly involved in the decisions.
Under the facts presented,
decisions involving the theater will not directly affect Mayor
Silveria’s source of income, real property or business entity.
(Regulation 18702.l.)
Similarly, the decisions will not directly
affect Councilmember Novoa’s leasehold interest or business
entity.
In all other cases, materiality is determined by applying an
indirect financial effect test.
It appears from the facts you
have provided that the decisions will affect the officials’
interests indirectly,
as discussed below.
1.
Mayor Silveria’s Real Property Interest
The effect of a decision on real property within 300 feet of
the property that is the subject of the decision is material if
“the decision will have some financial effect on the property.”
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (1).)
Mayor Silveria’s property is within 300 feet of the theater.
Consequently, since the mayor’s property is in such close
proximity to the property which is the subject of thedecision,
Mayor Silveria may not participate in any of the decisions unless
they will have no financial effect on his real property.
Please note,
a conflict of interest would exist where the
property was financially affected, whether the official’s decision
would increase or decrease the value of their real property.
For
example, if a decision reduces or increases traffic flow near the
mayor’s property, it is likely that the impact will have some
financial effect on the value of the property.
(See e.g.,
McClure
Advice Letter, A-95-024.)
It is the responsibility of the public official who owns the
property to ensure that the requirements of the Commission’s
materiality regulations are met.
It is to the benefit of the
official that a thorough assessment of financial effects is made
and that the facts and analysis on which the assessment is based,
It is important to note that Mayor Silveria will be
disqualified from any decisions involving the theater if his
construction company becomes directly involved in the project.
His company may become directly involved if it submits a bid or
otherwise gains a contract to perform áonstruction tasks on the
theater.
(Regulation 18702.1(b) (3).)


File No.
A—95—389
Page
7
including any appraisals,
are accurate and thoroughly documented.
(Diaz Advice Letter,
No.
A—95—143.)
2.
Mayor Silveria’s Source of Income
Mayor Silveria’s tenant is a source of income to him because
the tenant has paid him over $250 within the past twelve months.
(Section 18703(c).) If the tenant is an individual and is
occupying the property pursuant to a lease of more than one month,
the tenant has a leasehold interest in the property as defined by
Section 82033 and Regulation 18233.
A decision indirectly
involving the tenant will materially affect the tenant if the
decision would affect
(1)
the tenant’s income,
investments or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than
real property)
by $1,000 or more
(Regulation 18702.6(a));
(2)
the
legally allowable or actual use of the property;
(3)
the rent the
tenant pays by the greater of $250 or five percent, during any 12-
month period following the decision; or
(4)
the termination date
of the lease.
(Regulation 18702.4(a),
(b),
(d)
and (e).)
If the tenant is a business entity,
you must refer to the
tests set forth in Regulation 18702.2.
Regulation 18702.2
instructs the official to analyze the material effect of a
decision on.a business entity by considering the size of the
business involved.
For a relatively small business please refer
to Regulation 18702.2(g)
which provides that the effect is
material if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more;
or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more;
or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.
Mayor Silveria must determine whether each decision will have
a material financial effect on his tenant.
3.
Mayor Silveria and Councilmember Novoa’s Business
Entities
Mayor Silveria must consider whether his construction
business will be materially affected by any decisions involving
the theater by referring to the factors in Regulation 18702.2, set
forth above.
In addition, decisions involving the theater will have a
material financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s jewelry


Page8
business if any of the factors in Regulation 18702.2(g), set forth
above,
are met.
4.
Councilmeinber Novoa’s Leasehold Interest
Councilinember Novoa has a leasehold interest in property
within 300 feet of the theater.
The effect on Councjlmember
Novoa’s leasehold interest will be material if any of the
following applies:
(a)
The decision will change the legally allowable
use of the leased property,
and the lessee has a right
to sublease the property;
(b)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the lessee
will change the actual use of the property as a result
of the decision;
(c)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will result in a change in the actual use of property
within 300 feet of the leased property,
and the changed
use will significantly enhance or significantly decrease
the use or enjoyment of the leased property;
(d)
The decision will increase or decrease the
amount of rent for the leased property by $250 or
5
percent, whichever is greater, during any 12—month
period following the decision; or
(e)
The decision will result in a change in the
termination date of the lease.
(Regulation 18702. 4.)
D.
GOVERNMENTAL DECISIONS
Generally,
each governmental decision must be analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a reasonable material
financial effect on an official’s economic interests.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
The Commission does not act as a finder
of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
The official must carefully analyze each decision to
determine the effect on the official’s economic interests.
The
elected officials may have a conflict of interest in the project
as a whole, but some decisions may be merely implementation
decisions which may be separable from the decisions concerning the
project as a whole.
(Wachob Advice Letter,
No.
1—91—464.)
Thus,
the official must analyze each decision separately.
1.
May either official participate in decisions regarding
the Police Activity League’s maintenance of a small office in the
theater building?


rI.t iu.
i—-i—--o
Page
9
(a)
Mayor Silveria
To determine whether Mayor Silveria may participate in these
decisions,
you must consider whether locating the PAL office in
the theater building will foreseeably have any financial effect on
Mayor Silveria’s real property interests, pursuant to Regulation
18702
3 (a) (1).
In addition, you must determine whether the
decisions will foreseeably have a material financial effect on
Mayor Silveria’s economic interests pursuant to Regulations
18703(c)
and 18702.2.
In determining the effect on the mayor’s economic interests,
you should consider how and whether the decisions will affect such
issues as the traffic flow, parking and noise level near the
theater,
as well as any other impact the use of the theater might
have on the area.
On the facts you have provided here,
however, it does not
appear that there is a substantial likelihood that locating a
small office in the theater will have a financial effect on the
mayor’s real property,
source of income or business entity.
Consequently, absent other facts, it is not foreseeable that the
decisions will have a material financial effect on any of the
mayor’s economic interests.
(b)
Councilmember Novoa
The analysis for Councilmember Novoa’s economic interests is
similar to the analysis presented for Mayor Silveria.
You must
determine whether the decisions will have a foreseeable material
financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s leasehold interest or
business entity, pursuant to Regulations 18702.4 and 18702.2(g).
As noted above, there are no facts indicating that locating the
office in the theater will have any effect on the surrounding
neighborhood.
Therefore, it is probably not foreseeable that the
decisions will have a material financial effect on Councilmember
Novoa’s economic interests.
2.
May either official participate in decisions regarding
what type of programs the city will permit in the building?
(a)
and
(b)
Mayor Silveria and Counciliuember Novoa
You have not given sufficient facts to indicate how
decisions regarding the use of the theater may indirectly affect
the property in the area.
The uses considered for the theater may
vary widely in their effect on the surrounding neighborhood.
Absent additional information about the specific uses which are
being considered,
we cannot give you a complete answer on whether
either Mayor Silveria or Councilmember Novoa will be disqualified.
However, as discussed above, both offitials must apply the
statutes and regulations as set forth above to determine the
impact any of the city council’s decisions involving the use of
the theater may have on Mayor Silveria’s real property interest,


Page 10
source of income or business entity or Councilmember Novoa’s
V
leasehold interest or business entity.
3.
May the officials participate in decisions regarding the
interim use of the building?
(a)
and
(b)
Mayor Silveria and Councilmember Novoa
We do not have sufficient information to determine how the
interim uses may indirectly affect the surrounding neighborhood.
As noted in the conclusion to Decision 2,
you must apply the
materiality regulations to determine whether the decisions will
affect Mayor Silveria’s real property interest,
source of income
or business entity or Councilmember Novoa’s leasehold interest or
business entity.
4.
May either official participate in a decision regarding
payment of the existing trust deed or from where the payment
should come?
(a)
Mayor Silveria
V
In decisions pertaining to financing a project, the
Commission has advised that once the “basic policy decisions” to
build or maintain have been reached, the official
“may participate
in the decisions which implement,
but do not change these
policies.”
(Athan Advice Letter,
No.
A—86—094,
copy enclosed.)
In the Martello Advice Letter,
No. A-92-471
(copy enclosed)
we
advised that an official could participate in a decision regarding
the financing of a theater complex to the extent that the decision
did not affect the existence of the project as a whole and would
not independently materially affect an economic interest of the
official.
In this instance,
you indicated that the payment of the
trust deed will not affect the city’s ownership of the theater and
that defaulting on the deed is not an option.
If that is the
case,
Mayor Silveria may participate in the decision regarding
payment of the trust deed,
so long as the decision does not
independently materially affect any of his financial interests.
(b)
Councilmeiuber Novoa
The analysis is the same for Councilmember Novoa.
Your facts
indicate that the decision is merely an implementation decision,
Councilmember Novoa may participate so long as the decision does
not independently materially affect any of his financial
interests.
5.
May either official participate in decisions regarding
what improvements are to be made to the theater?
I


Page 11
(a)
Mayor Silveria
As discussed above, if the decision is merely an
implementation of the city’s policy decision to retain and operate
the theater,
then Mayor Silveria will not have a conflict of
interest in the decision.
We do not have sufficient information
to render advice on the maintenance decisions being considered.
Basic maintenance of the theater,
such as the new roof and the new
air conditioner you mentioned, will probably be considered
implementation decisions.
Should the city consider improvements
which go beyond the maintenance of the theater, for example,
policy decisions which impact either the appearance or use of the
theater, the implementation exception may not apply.
Mayor
Silveria must carefully consider each decision to determine
whether it will have a material financial effect on his business
entity or tenant or whether it will have any financial effect on
Mayor Silveria’s real property interests.
In addition,
Mayor
Silveria will be required to disqualify himself if his
construction business becomes directly involved in any of the
improvements,
such as submitting a bid to perform construction
tasks.
(b)
Councilmember Novoa
The analysis is substantially the same as for Mayor Silveria.
Councilmember Novoa may carefully consider each improvement
decision.
If it is an implementation decision,
he may
participate.
If it is a policy decision,
he must consider whether
it will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
his business entity or leasehold interest.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Liane Randolph
Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak

1.
Neither official may participate in any decisions
regarding the theater if the decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on their economic interests.
From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there is
a substantial likelihood that permitting the PAL organization to
maintain a small office will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on either of the officials’ economic interests.
2 and 3.
Neither official may participate in decisions
regarding the type of programs the city will permit in the theater
if the programs will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on their economic interests.
We do not have
sufficient information about the uses being considered to give you
formal advice on this issue,
however,
we have provided guidance to
assist you in analyzing the issue.
2
The officials must carefully
analyze the impact of these decisions.
If the decisions will have
any financial effect on Mayor Silveria’s real property interest
within 300 feet of the theater,
he may not participate and if the
decisions will have a material financial effect on Mayor
Silveria’s tenant or business entity,
he may not participate.
Similarly, if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s tenant or
leasehold interest, then Councilmeniber Novoa may not participate
in the decisions.
4.
We do not have sufficient information about the
improvements being considered to give you formal advice as to
whether the officials may participate.
To the extent that
decisions pertaining to the theater are merely implementation
decisions which do not affect the existence of the project as a
whole and will not independently materially affect an economic
interest of the officials, the officials may participate.
5.
As noted above, the officials may not participate in
decisions regarding the theater if the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their economic
interests.
According to the facts you have provided, it appears
that the decision on the trust deed is an implementation decision
2

informal assistance does not provide the reguestor
with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
which will not affect the existence of the project.
So long as
the decision will not independently materially affect an economic
interest of the officials, the officials may both participate.
PACTS
The City of Newman recently acquired the West Side Theater
through a Trustee’s Sale from a second deed of trust.
Mayor James
Silveria owns real property within 300 feet of the theater.
He
has a tenant located in his real property.
In addition, Mayor
Silveria maintains his construction business in the property.
City Councilmember Robert Novoa operates a jewelry store in
part of a building which he has leased.
The building is within
300 feet of the theater.
The theater will be used for certain city functions, although
the exact uses and the terms of such uses have yet to be
established.
An advisory committee was appointed to make
recommendations and report to the city council regarding the
theater’s use.
The report was accepted at the last city council
meeting, but Councilmember Novoa and Mayor Silveria abstained from
the decision.

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or iore in value
provided to, received by or promied to the public


—--—-----
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103.)
The mayor and councilmeinber have a variety of interests that
may be affected by decisions regarding the theater.
If any of the
decisions involving the theater reasonably and foreseeably will
have a material financial effect on the officials’
economic
interests, the officials must disqualify themselves from the
decisions.
In Part A of this letter, I will identify the economic
interests held by Mayor Silveria and Councilmember Novoa which may
be affected by decisions regarding the theater.
In Parts
B and C,
I will set forth the Commission’s regulations the officials will
need to apply to determine whether any decisions will foreseeably
and materially affect their financial interests.
In Part D,
I
will provide advice on how to apply the materiality regulations to
determine if the officials must disqualify themselves from any
decisions involving the theater.
A.
ECONOMIC
INTERESTS
1.
Mayor Silveria
Mayor Silveria has three potential interests in decisions
involving the theater.
(a)
Mayor Silveria has real property,
presumably worth more
than $1,000,
located within 300 feet of the theater.
(Section
87103(b).)
(b)
Mayor Silveria’s construction business is located in
his building near the theater.
(Section 87103(a).)
If the mayor
has an investment worth more than $1,000 in his construction
business,
he must analyze whether the decisions will reasonably
foreseeably and materially affect his business entity.
(c)
Mayor Silveria has a tenant in the building and that
tenant is a source of income to Mayor Silveria if the mayor has
received more than $250 from the tenant within the past 12 months.
(Section 87103 (c).)
2.
Councilniember Novoa
Councilmember Novoa has two interests which may be affected
by decisions regarding the theater.
(a)
Couricilmember Novoa has a real property interest in
the form of a leasehold,
assuming that his lease is more than a
month-to-month tenancy, in a building within 300 feet of the
theater.
(Section 82033; Regulation 1233.)


FIleNô. A9b-39
(b)
Councilmember Novoa’s jewelry business is located in
the leased property near the theater.
If the councilmenther has an
investment worth more than $1,000 in his construction business,
he
must analyze whether the decisions will reasonably foreseeably and
materially affect his business entity.
(Section 18703(a).)
Thus,
each official is required to disqualify himself from
making or participating in any decisions which would have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on the official’s
economic interest.
B.
FORESEEABILITY
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there isa substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
C.
MATERIALITY
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which
provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial
effects of a decision are material.
These regulations apply
different standards depending on whether the decision will
directly or indirectly affect the official’s economic interest.
3
The effect of a decision on real property or a business
entity in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial
ownership interest,
is material if the official’s property is
directly involved in a decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3).)
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3)
provides a list of situations in
which an official’s real property is directly involved in a
decision.
Some examples include decisions to zone or rezone the
official’s property, decisions to annex .the property, decisions
concerning assessments on the property and decisions to include
the property in or exclude it from any city, county or other local
governmental decision.
Regulation 18703 provides an exception where the material
financial effect on the official’s interest is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.)
Since the official’s property is so close to
the theater, it does not appear that the test for the exception
will be met.
However,
we have enclosed the regulation for your
consideration.
If you do believe the 1
’public generally” exception
will apply or if you have any further questions, please feel free
to contact us at
(916)
322—5660.


•
File No
The effect of a decision on a business entity is material i
the business entity initiates the proceeding, is a named party in
the proceeding or if the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of a permit,
license or contract.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
The officials will be disqualified if their property is
directly involved in the decisions.
Under the facts presented,
decisions involving the theater will not directly affect Mayor
Silveria’s source of income, real property or business entity.
(Regulation 18702.l.)
Similarly, the decisions will not directly
affect Councilmember Novoa’s leasehold interest or business
entity.
In all other cases, materiality is determined by applying an
indirect financial effect test.
It appears from the facts you
have provided that the decisions will affect the officials’
interests indirectly,
as discussed below.
1.
Mayor Silveria’s Real Property Interest
The effect of a decision on real property within 300 feet of
the property that is the subject of the decision is material if
“the decision will have some financial effect on the property.”
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (1).)
Mayor Silveria’s property is within 300 feet of the theater.
Consequently, since the mayor’s property is in such close
proximity to the property which is the subject of thedecision,
Mayor Silveria may not participate in any of the decisions unless
they will have no financial effect on his real property.
Please note,
a conflict of interest would exist where the
property was financially affected, whether the official’s decision
would increase or decrease the value of their real property.
For
example, if a decision reduces or increases traffic flow near the
mayor’s property, it is likely that the impact will have some
financial effect on the value of the property.
(See e.g.,
McClure
Advice Letter, A-95-024.)
It is the responsibility of the public official who owns the
property to ensure that the requirements of the Commission’s
materiality regulations are met.
It is to the benefit of the
official that a thorough assessment of financial effects is made
and that the facts and analysis on which the assessment is based,
It is important to note that Mayor Silveria will be
disqualified from any decisions involving the theater if his
construction company becomes directly involved in the project.
His company may become directly involved if it submits a bid or
otherwise gains a contract to perform áonstruction tasks on the
theater.
(Regulation 18702.1(b) (3).)


File No.
A—95—389
including any appraisals,
are accurate and thoroughly documented.
(Diaz Advice Letter,
No.
A—95—143.)
2.
Mayor Silveria’s Source of Income
Mayor Silveria’s tenant is a source of income to him because
the tenant has paid him over $250 within the past twelve months.
(Section 18703(c).) If the tenant is an individual and is
occupying the property pursuant to a lease of more than one month,
the tenant has a leasehold interest in the property as defined by
Section 82033 and Regulation 18233.
A decision indirectly
involving the tenant will materially affect the tenant if the
decision would affect
(1)
the tenant’s income,
investments or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than
real property)
by $1,000 or more
(Regulation 18702.6(a));
(2)
the
legally allowable or actual use of the property;
(3)
the rent the
tenant pays by the greater of $250 or five percent, during any 12-
month period following the decision; or
(4)
the termination date
of the lease.
(Regulation 18702.4(a),
(b),
(d)
and (e).)
If the tenant is a business entity,
you must refer to the
tests set forth in Regulation 18702.2.
Regulation 18702.2
instructs the official to analyze the material effect of a
decision on.a business entity by considering the size of the
business involved.
For a relatively small business please refer
to Regulation 18702.2(g)
which provides that the effect is
material if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more;
or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal
year in the amount of $2,500 or more;
or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.
Mayor Silveria must determine whether each decision will have
a material financial effect on his tenant.
3.
Mayor Silveria and Councilmember Novoa’s Business
Entities
Mayor Silveria must consider whether his construction
business will be materially affected by any decisions involving
the theater by referring to the factors in Regulation 18702.2, set
forth above.
In addition, decisions involving the theater will have a
material financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s jewelry


Page8
business if any of the factors in Regulation 18702.2(g), set forth
above,
are met.
4.
Councilmeinber Novoa’s Leasehold Interest
Councilinember Novoa has a leasehold interest in property
within 300 feet of the theater.
The effect on Councjlmember
Novoa’s leasehold interest will be material if any of the
following applies:
(a)
The decision will change the legally allowable
use of the leased property,
and the lessee has a right
to sublease the property;
(b)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the lessee
will change the actual use of the property as a result
of the decision;
(c)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will result in a change in the actual use of property
within 300 feet of the leased property,
and the changed
use will significantly enhance or significantly decrease
the use or enjoyment of the leased property;
(d)
The decision will increase or decrease the
amount of rent for the leased property by $250 or
5
percent, whichever is greater, during any 12—month
period following the decision; or
(e)
The decision will result in a change in the
termination date of the lease.
(Regulation 18702. 4.)
D.
GOVERNMENTAL DECISIONS
Generally,
each governmental decision must be analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a reasonable material
financial effect on an official’s economic interests.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
The Commission does not act as a finder
of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
The official must carefully analyze each decision to
determine the effect on the official’s economic interests.
The
elected officials may have a conflict of interest in the project
as a whole, but some decisions may be merely implementation
decisions which may be separable from the decisions concerning the
project as a whole.
(Wachob Advice Letter,
No.
1—91—464.)
Thus,
the official must analyze each decision separately.
1.
May either official participate in decisions regarding
the Police Activity League’s maintenance of a small office in the
theater building?


rI.t iu.
i—-i—--o
(a)
Mayor Silveria
To determine whether Mayor Silveria may participate in these
decisions,
you must consider whether locating the PAL office in
the theater building will foreseeably have any financial effect on
Mayor Silveria’s real property interests, pursuant to Regulation
18702
3 (a) (1).
In addition, you must determine whether the
decisions will foreseeably have a material financial effect on
Mayor Silveria’s economic interests pursuant to Regulations
18703(c)
and 18702.2.
In determining the effect on the mayor’s economic interests,
you should consider how and whether the decisions will affect such
issues as the traffic flow, parking and noise level near the
theater,
as well as any other impact the use of the theater might
have on the area.
On the facts you have provided here,
however, it does not
appear that there is a substantial likelihood that locating a
small office in the theater will have a financial effect on the
mayor’s real property,
source of income or business entity.
Consequently, absent other facts, it is not foreseeable that the
decisions will have a material financial effect on any of the
mayor’s economic interests.
(b)
Councilmember Novoa
The analysis for Councilmember Novoa’s economic interests is
similar to the analysis presented for Mayor Silveria.
You must
determine whether the decisions will have a foreseeable material
financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s leasehold interest or
business entity, pursuant to Regulations 18702.4 and 18702.2(g).
As noted above, there are no facts indicating that locating the
office in the theater will have any effect on the surrounding
neighborhood.
Therefore, it is probably not foreseeable that the
decisions will have a material financial effect on Councilmember
Novoa’s economic interests.
2.
May either official participate in decisions regarding
what type of programs the city will permit in the building?
(a)
and
(b)
Mayor Silveria and Counciliuember Novoa
You have not given sufficient facts to indicate how
decisions regarding the use of the theater may indirectly affect
the property in the area.
The uses considered for the theater may
vary widely in their effect on the surrounding neighborhood.
Absent additional information about the specific uses which are
being considered,
we cannot give you a complete answer on whether
either Mayor Silveria or Councilmember Novoa will be disqualified.
However, as discussed above, both offitials must apply the
statutes and regulations as set forth above to determine the
impact any of the city council’s decisions involving the use of
the theater may have on Mayor Silveria’s real property interest,
source of income or business entity or Councilmember Novoa’s
V
leasehold interest or business entity.
3.
May the officials participate in decisions regarding the
interim use of the building?
(a)
and
(b)
Mayor Silveria and Councilmember Novoa
We do not have sufficient information to determine how the
interim uses may indirectly affect the surrounding neighborhood.
As noted in the conclusion to Decision 2,
you must apply the
materiality regulations to determine whether the decisions will
affect Mayor Silveria’s real property interest,
source of income
or business entity or Councilmember Novoa’s leasehold interest or
business entity.
4.
May either official participate in a decision regarding
payment of the existing trust deed or from where the payment
should come?
(a)
Mayor Silveria
V
In decisions pertaining to financing a project, the
Commission has advised that once the “basic policy decisions” to
build or maintain have been reached, the official
“may participate
in the decisions which implement,
but do not change these
policies.”
(Athan Advice Letter,
No.
A—86—094,
copy enclosed.)
In the Martello Advice Letter,
No. A-92-471
(copy enclosed)
we
advised that an official could participate in a decision regarding
the financing of a theater complex to the extent that the decision
did not affect the existence of the project as a whole and would
not independently materially affect an economic interest of the
official.
In this instance,
you indicated that the payment of the
trust deed will not affect the city’s ownership of the theater and
that defaulting on the deed is not an option.
If that is the
case,
Mayor Silveria may participate in the decision regarding
payment of the trust deed,
so long as the decision does not
independently materially affect any of his financial interests.
(b)
Councilmeiuber Novoa
The analysis is the same for Councilmember Novoa.
Your facts
indicate that the decision is merely an implementation decision,
Councilmember Novoa may participate so long as the decision does
not independently materially affect any of his financial
interests.
5.
May either official participate in decisions regarding
what improvements are to be made to the theater?
I
(a)
Mayor Silveria
As discussed above, if the decision is merely an
implementation of the city’s policy decision to retain and operate
the theater,
then Mayor Silveria will not have a conflict of
interest in the decision.
We do not have sufficient information
to render advice on the maintenance decisions being considered.
Basic maintenance of the theater,
such as the new roof and the new
air conditioner you mentioned, will probably be considered
implementation decisions.
Should the city consider improvements
which go beyond the maintenance of the theater, for example,
policy decisions which impact either the appearance or use of the
theater, the implementation exception may not apply.
Mayor
Silveria must carefully consider each decision to determine
whether it will have a material financial effect on his business
entity or tenant or whether it will have any financial effect on
Mayor Silveria’s real property interests.
In addition,
Mayor
Silveria will be required to disqualify himself if his
construction business becomes directly involved in any of the
improvements,
such as submitting a bid to perform construction
tasks.
(b)
Councilmember Novoa
The analysis is substantially the same as for Mayor Silveria.
Councilmember Novoa may carefully consider each improvement
decision.
If it is an implementation decision,
he may
participate.
If it is a policy decision,
he must consider whether
it will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
his business entity or leasehold interest.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding the Police Activity League’s
(“PAL”)
proposed use of a
theater the city has recently acquired?
PAL,
which provides
activities for city and area youth, wishes to maintain a small
office in the theater building.
2.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding what type of programs the city will permit in the
building (i.e., movies, plays, musicals,
PAL events, craft fairs,
etc.)?
3.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding the interim use of the building (i.e., nonprofit groups,
social events)?
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Cod,e of Regulations, Sections
18000—18995.

(916) 322-5660


-
4.
May either official participate in the decisions
regarding what improvements are to be made to the theater,
Such as
roof repair or a new air conditioner,
and is there a distinction
if the improvements are visible from the street?
5.
May either official participate in the decision regarding
payment of the existing trust deed or from where the payment
should come?

CONCLUSION: 1.
Neither official may participate in any decisions
regarding the theater if the decisions will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on their economic interests.
From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there is
a substantial likelihood that permitting the PAL organization to
maintain a small office will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on either of the officials’ economic interests.
2 and 3.
Neither official may participate in decisions
regarding the type of programs the city will permit in the theater
if the programs will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on their economic interests.
We do not have
sufficient information about the uses being considered to give you
formal advice on this issue,
however,
we have provided guidance to
assist you in analyzing the issue.
2
The officials must carefully
analyze the impact of these decisions.
If the decisions will have
any financial effect on Mayor Silveria’s real property interest
within 300 feet of the theater,
he may not participate and if the
decisions will have a material financial effect on Mayor
Silveria’s tenant or business entity,
he may not participate.
Similarly, if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s tenant or
leasehold interest, then Councilmeniber Novoa may not participate
in the decisions.
4.
We do not have sufficient information about the
improvements being considered to give you formal advice as to
whether the officials may participate.
To the extent that
decisions pertaining to the theater are merely implementation
decisions which do not affect the existence of the project as a
whole and will not independently materially affect an economic
interest of the officials, the officials may participate.
5.
As noted above, the officials may not participate in
decisions regarding the theater if the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their economic
interests.
According to the facts you have provided, it appears
that the decision on the trust deed is an implementation decision
2
Please note,
informal assistance does not provide the reguestor
with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
which will not affect the existence of the project.
So long as
the decision will not independently materially affect an economic
interest of the officials, the officials may both participate.
PACTS
The City of Newman recently acquired the West Side Theater
through a Trustee’s Sale from a second deed of trust.
Mayor James
Silveria owns real property within 300 feet of the theater.
He
has a tenant located in his real property.
In addition, Mayor
Silveria maintains his construction business in the property.
City Councilmember Robert Novoa operates a jewelry store in
part of a building which he has leased.
The building is within
300 feet of the theater.
The theater will be used for certain city functions, although
the exact uses and the terms of such uses have yet to be
established.
An advisory committee was appointed to make
recommendations and report to the city council regarding the
theater’s use.
The report was accepted at the last city council
meeting, but Councilmember Novoa and Mayor Silveria abstained from
the decision.

Waggoner Law Offices of Waggoner & Loeffler 318 McHenry Avenue Modesto, California 95354 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A—95-389 Dear Mr. Waggoner: You are the City Attorney for the City of Newman. This is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict—of— interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) as they pertain to City of Newman Mayor James Silveria and Councilinember Robert Novoa. 1 QUESTIONS 1. May either official participate in the decisions regarding the Police Activity League’s (“PAL”) proposed use of a theater the city has recently acquired? PAL, which provides activities for city and area youth, wishes to maintain a small office in the theater building. 2. May either official participate in the decisions regarding what type of programs the city will permit in the building (i.e., movies, plays, musicals, PAL events, craft fairs, etc.)? 3. May either official participate in the decisions regarding the interim use of the building (i.e., nonprofit groups, social events)? 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Cod,e of Regulations, Sections 18000—18995. 428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660 - Page 2 4. May either official participate in the decisions regarding what improvements are to be made to the theater, Such as roof repair or a new air conditioner, and is there a distinction if the improvements are visible from the street? 5. May either official participate in the decision regarding payment of the existing trust deed or from where the payment should come? CONCLUSIONS 1. Neither official may participate in any decisions regarding the theater if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their economic interests. From the facts you have provided, it does not appear that there is a substantial likelihood that permitting the PAL organization to maintain a small office will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on either of the officials’ economic interests. 2 and 3. Neither official may participate in decisions regarding the type of programs the city will permit in the theater if the programs will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their economic interests. We do not have sufficient information about the uses being considered to give you formal advice on this issue, however, we have provided guidance to assist you in analyzing the issue. 2 The officials must carefully analyze the impact of these decisions. If the decisions will have any financial effect on Mayor Silveria’s real property interest within 300 feet of the theater, he may not participate and if the decisions will have a material financial effect on Mayor Silveria’s tenant or business entity, he may not participate. Similarly, if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Novoa’s tenant or leasehold interest, then Councilmeniber Novoa may not participate in the decisions. 4. We
